Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Board has 3 panels: top-left (black bg with white+purple rects), top-right (gray bg with small indicator boxes), main area (black bg with gray-bordered purple rect + white rect below) - Bottom row 63 is a charcoal strip that appears to be a timer/HUD (changed 1 cell at corner after my click) - Clicking the purple rectangle in main area had no gameplay effect (only HUD changed) - Goal unclear yet - possibly to rearrange objects to match top-left pattern (white above purple)
- Plan: Try SPACE and arrow keys to discover interaction mechanics.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model.

When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Again only 1 cell changed. Let me check if it's the same cell as before (the timer). I'm wasting actions by clicking randomly. Let me step back and think more carefully.
The initial state had:
- Top-left: white rect above purple rect (target pattern?)
- Top-right: two small boxes with white and purple squares
- Main: purple rect (in gray frame) above white rect
After SPACE: the white rect in main area split into purple (top half) + white (bottom half)
Now the main area has: purple, purple, white (three rects)
The top-left has: white, purple (two rects)
I've been wasting actions. Let me try to understand the top-right panel better - maybe those small boxes are interactive elements that control what color SPACE uses, or they're indicators of progress.
Let me look at the initial frame more carefully and compare to now.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me look at the very first frame (initial state) vs now
initial_frame = history[0].frame if history else current_frame
print("Initial frame nodes:")
for n in initial_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
    print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

print("\nCurrent frame nodes:")
for n in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
    print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Check what the last action changed
before = last_transition.before_frame
after = last_transition.after_frame
before_lines = before.ascii.split('\n')
after_lines = after.ascii.split('\n')

changed = []
for r in range(64):
    for c in range(64):
        if before_lines[r][c] != after_lines[r][c]:
            changed.append((r, c, before_lines[r][c], after_lines[r][c]))
print(f"\nLast action changed: {changed}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK so the last action only changed the timer bar (charcoal strip at row 63). The white rectangle I clicked on didn't change at all. All my clicks so far have only affected the timer bar.
Let me reconsider. The initial state has:
- Top-left panel: white rect (5x10) above purple rect (5x10)
- Top-right panel: two small boxes - left one has white square, right one has purple square, with a white line below the purple box
- Main area: gray frame containing purple rect (7x12), below it a white rect (10x10)
After SPACE: the white rect split into purple (5x10) and white (5x10)
So now main area has: purple (7x12 in frame), purple (5x10), white (5x10)
And top-left has: white (5x10), purple (5x10)
The top-right panel shows white and purple as color options. Maybe I need to:
1. Select a color from the top-right panel
2. Then click on objects to paint them
3. Goal is to make the main area match the top-left pattern
But clicking on objects doesn't seem to change them. Let me try clicking on the top-right panel's color boxes to select a color, then try SPACE or clicking again.
Actually wait - let me look at the top-right more carefully. There's a white line (id=8, 5 pixels) at row 7, cols 41-45. This is below the purple box. Maybe this is a "cursor" indicating which color is currently selected. Currently it's below the purple box, meaning purple is selected.
Let me try clicking on the white box to select white, then use SPACE to paint.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model:
- Board has 3 panels: top-left (target pattern: white above purple), top-right (color selector with white/purple boxes + indicator line), main area (gray-framed purple rect + purple rect + white rect after SPACE)
- Bottom row 63 is a HUD timer (shrinks each action)
- SPACE splits the bottom rectangle in half and colors the top half with the selected color
- Top-right indicator line (white, 5px) is below the purple box, suggesting purple is selected
- Clicking on main area objects has no gameplay effect (only timer changes)
- Goal: make main area match top-left pattern (white above purple)
- Plan: Try clicking top-right color boxes to switch selection, then use SPACE
